message.abstract:
<jats:p>
                  We constructed a simple Susceptible–Infected–Infectious–Excluded model of the spread of COVID-19. The model is parametrised only by the average incubation period,
                  <jats:italic>τ</jats:italic>
                  , and two rate parameters: contact rate,
                  <jats:italic>r</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>C</jats:sub>
                  , and exclusion rate,
                  <jats:italic>r</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>E</jats:sub>
                  . The rates can be manipulated by non-therapeutic interventions and determine the basic reproduction number,
                  <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                  =
                  <jats:italic>r</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>C</jats:sub>
                  /
                  <jats:italic>r</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>E</jats:sub>
                  , and, together with
                  <jats:italic>τ</jats:italic>
                  , the daily multiplication coefficient at the early exponential phase,
                  <jats:italic>β</jats:italic>
                  . Initial
                  <jats:italic>β</jats:italic>
                  determines the reduction of
                  <jats:italic>r</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>C</jats:sub>
                  required to contain epidemic spread. In the long-term, we consider a scenario based on typical social behaviours, in which
                  <jats:italic>r</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>C</jats:sub>
                  first decreases in response to a surge of daily new cases, forcing people to self-isolate, and then slowly increases when people gradually accept higher risk. Consequently, initial abrupt epidemic spread is followed by a plateau and slow regression. This scenario, although economically and socially devastating, will grant time to develop, produce, and distribute a vaccine, or at least limit daily cases to a manageable number.
                </jats:p>
title: Dynamics of COVID-19 pandemic at constant and time-dependent contact rates

reference.unstructured: N. M. Linton , T. Kobayashi , Y. Yang , K. Hayashi , A. R. Akhmetzhanov , S.-M. Jung , B. Yuan , R. Kinoshita , and H. Nishiura , Journal of Clinical Medicine 9 (2020).
R. M. Anderson and R. M. May , Infectious Diseases of Humans: Dynamics and Control (Oxford University Press, New York, 1991).
“Novel coronavirus (COVID-19) cases, provided by JHU CSSE,” https://systems.jhu.edu/research/public-health/ncov, accessed: 2020-03-20.
A. J. Kucharski , T. W. Russell , C. Diamond , Y. Liu , J. Edmunds , S. Funk , and R. M. Eggo , Lancet. Infectious diseases (2020).
“How many tests for covid-19 are being performed around the world?” https://ourworldindata.org/covid-testing, accessed: 2020-03-20.

reference.journal-title: Journal of the Royal Society Interface
